quote: Take courage; it is I. Do not be afraid.
firstReading: In Acts 6:1-7 the early Church faces a practical crisis: Greek-speaking widows are being neglected in the daily distribution. The Twelve appoint seven men of good reputation and filled with the Spirit to oversee this ministry, so the apostles can remain focused on prayer and the ministry of the word. The solution strengthens internal order, widens participation in service, and results in the continued growth of the community and the spread of the word.
psalmSummary: Psalm 33 calls the faithful to rejoice in the Lord’s justice and creative power. It praises God’s reliable word and faithful judgment, inviting a response of glad praise. The psalmist affirms that the Lord watches over those who fear him and places hope in his unfailing love; God’s protective gaze sustains the poor and preserves the lives of those who trust in him.
gospelSummary: John 6:16-21 describes the disciples crossing the Sea of Galilee at night when a sudden wind-tossed storm frightens them. Midway through the crossing, they see Jesus walking on the sea toward them. The disciples are terrified, but Jesus speaks to them—his presence calms their fear. They receive him into the boat, and immediately the boat reaches the shore where they were headed. The episode underscores Jesus’ sovereignty over creation and his power to dispel fear through tangible presence.
saintReflection: Blessed James Oldo (Optional Memorial): Blessed James Oldo (1364-1404) was a wealthy Italian layman whose life was transformed when he witnessed a traveling preacher and reflected on his own mortality during an outbreak of plague. He and his wife embraced the Franciscan Third Order, turning their lavish home into a chapel and dedicating their wealth to the sick and imprisoned.
dailyPrayer: Lord Jesus, when life’s winds and waves unsettle us, come walking into our fear and calm our hearts. Give us the wisdom to see where practical service is needed and the courage to entrust those tasks to humble hands, so that pastoral leadership can give itself to prayer and the word. Bless our efforts to serve the poor and the sick, and make our possessions signs of mercy. Like Blessed James Oldo, may we turn fear of death into compassionate action, offering shelter and consolation to those in need. Keep us mindful of your abiding presence; teach us to say with confidence and peace, “It is I; do not be afraid.” Strengthen us in faith, hope, and charity, that your Church may grow in holiness. Amen.
theologicalSynthesis: The readings gather around two complementary truths: the ordering of charity and the comforting presence of Christ. Acts 6 models how communal structures protect the mission: appointing servants for the needy frees apostles for prayer and proclamation, showing that charity and Word ministry are mutually enabling. Psalm 33 celebrates God’s dependable care and providential watchfulness, the theological ground for Christian hope. In John 6, Christ’s walking on the sea and his words, “Do not be afraid,” reveal that God’s power governs chaos and transforms fear into trust. Blessed James Oldo embodies these converging themes: his conversion led to organized charity rooted in prayerful dependence on God. Practically, the texts call the community to combine prayerful leadership with accountable service, trusting that Christ’s presence guides and sustains us amid storms.
exegesis:
Intro: These readings, chosen for this weekday in the Easter season, form a coherent pastoral and spiritual program. Acts 6:1-7 addresses community life and ministry; Psalm 33 provides a theological backdrop of trust in God’s providence; John 6:16-21 gives a concrete, sacramental image of Christ’s presence in danger. Together they instruct both the internal organization of the Church and the interior disposition of the faithful: service ordered by prayer, and faith replacing fear.

Context: Acts 6 sits at a pivotal moment in Luke-Acts. The Christian community in Jerusalem is growing rapidly; demographic and linguistic diversity introduces tensions. The complaint about neglected Greek-speaking widows is not merely administrative; it threatens unity and witness. The apostles’ response—delegating distribution to seven reputable men—establishes a pattern: the ministry of charity requires capable stewardship distinct from apostolic proclamation. Luke thus preserves a theology of ministry balance: proclamation (kerygma) and liturgical/practical care are both essential for a healthy ecclesial body. The result, explicitly stated, is growth: “the word of God increased,” and even some priests become obedient to the faith, showing the efficacy of ordered charity.

Psalm: Psalm 33 functions as a hymn of trust and a theological lens for interpreting events. Its celebration of God’s creative word and just governance reassures the community that the Lord’s providence is reliable. Verses 18–19 highlight that the Lord’s attentive care rests especially on those who fear him and who hope in his mercy. For a needy, newly forming church, this psalm encourages confidence: when proper structures are set up to care for the vulnerable, the community acts under God’s watchful eye. The psalm’s emphasis on God’s faithful counsel also undergirds the apostles’ decision to choose servants filled with spirit and wisdom—leaders whose character aligns with the Lord’s protective gaze.

Gospel: John 6:16-21 narrates a theologically rich, compact miracle. The motif is familiar: Jesus walks on the sea, evoking the divine mastery of chaotic waters (a recurrent biblical symbol for disorder). The disciples’ terror gives place to Jesus’ calming presence and voice. John’s detail that they intended to “harbor on the other side” and that the boat immediately reached shore once Jesus entered links the event to the eucharistic context of John 6: the multiplication of loaves and the discourse that follows. Here, Jesus’ presence in the boat is not merely miraculous athleticism but a revelation of his identity—God with us, who enters human vulnerability to dispel fear. The scene reassures the community that even amid tumult, Christ’s presence ensures deliverance.

Saints: Blessed James Oldo provides a concrete medieval witness to these scriptural dynamics. His conversion during a plague and subsequent decision to join the Franciscan Third Order turned private wealth into public charity. Like the appointed deacons, James reconfigured resources to serve the sick and imprisoned—those most at risk. The saint’s life shows how personal conversion effects structural change: hospitality became a form of evangelization, and material goods were redirected to embody God’s provident care celebrated by Psalm 33. His biography thereby illumines how sacramental trust in Christ’s presence moves believers from private comfort to communal service.

Today: For contemporary pastoral application, these texts challenge both institutions and individuals. Churches must design ministries that allow proclamation and prayer to flourish alongside effective social outreach. Administratively, appointing capable, Spirit-filled leaders for service preserves the apostles’ primary tasks. Spiritually, John’s scene beckons believers to recognize Christ’s presence in trials—reminding congregations that fear can be met by prayer and the tangible practice of charity. Blessed James’ example exhorts Christians to transform possessions into solidarity with the poor. Finally, Psalm 33 invites a posture of watchful hope: in reorganizing ministries and serving the vulnerable, the Church acts confident that the Lord watches over those who place their hope in him.
